figures.caption: Figure 8: Pareto fronts of feasible samples (tolerance e < 5×10−5 NU) obtained with homotopy and a gradient-based solver.
Table 2: Input parameters for the different MCMC algorithms. Values in brackets indicate adapted parameters for the final iterations with smaller stepsizes.
Figure 9: Mean values of the objective function, its three components, and the acceptance rate over the RWM iterations.
Figure 5: Jk and J∗ for a randomly selected sample λk. The objective values are plotted along the line λk + s∇Jk(λ)|λ=λk , where s ∈ R. The right panel shows a close-up near s = 0.

Table 3: Comparison of performance and computational cost for the three MCMC algorithms. Mean and standard deviation (STD) are reported for both objectives; feasibility rate is based on e < 5 × 10−5. All metrics are based on samples collected after the burn-in phase (15, 000 to 25, 000 per method). Arrows indicate whether larger (↑) or smaller (↓) values are preferred.
Figure 12: Mean values of the objective function, its three components, and acceptance rate over the HMC iterations.
Figure 6: Flowchart of variables and equations for gradient-based MCMC sampling.

Figure 15: The 19, 000 final MALA samples (left), 50, 000 samples from the fine-tuned DM (middle), and a subset of 21, 000 feasible DM samples (right) with e < 5× 10−5 displayed in the costate space for α = 1.0

Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the global search problem for indirect LT trajectory optimization under a varying mission parameter α. Clusters in costate space correspond to families of locally optimal trajectories, shown here for an example planar DRO-to-DRO transfer in the Jupiter–Europa system. Learning a conditional sampling distribution over these cluster enables efficient generation of new trajectory candidates across parameter values.

Figure 2: Comparison of the previous diffusion model framework4 (left) and the proposed transfer-learning extension (right). The illustration is schematic; in the actual indirect trajectory-design problem, the samples for each mission parameter α correspond to multimodal, non-Gaussian distributions in costate space.
Figure 14: Feasible samples (tolerance e < 5× 10−5 NU) from different approaches, shown in the ∆v–τs plane.

Figure 16: Pareto front in the objective frame obtained by warm-starting SNOPT with samples from the fine-tuned diffusion model for α = 1.0 (left). The Hamiltonian values of the Pareto-optimal samples are shown on the right.
Table 1: Problem parameters for Europa and Titan DRO transfers.
Figure 11: Mean values of the objective function, its three components, and acceptance rate over the MALA iterations.

Figure 3: Reformulation of optimization as sampling: minima of J∗(λ) correspond to peaks of the unnormalized target density π(λ) ≡ exp(−βJ∗(λ)). The scaling factor β controls the width and sharpness of the peaks.

Table 4: Comparison of performance and computational cost for the best of our MCMC approaches (MALA) against a state-of-the-art approach from the literature.8 Mean and standard deviation (STD) are reported for both objectives; feasibility rate is based on e < 5× 10−5. For MALA, ∆v and τs metrics are based on feasible samples collected after the burn-in phase. Arrows indicate whether larger (↑) or smaller (↓) values are preferred.
Figure 10: Comparison of RWM samples in the costate space for different values of α.
Figure 7: Representative locally optimal trajectories for the Europa DRO transfer (left) and Titan DRO transfer (right).
Figure 13: Feasible samples (tolerance e < 5× 10−5 NU) from RWM, MALA, and HMC, shown in the ∆v–τs plane.

Figure 4: Visualization of the forward and reverse diffusion processes for an indirect spacecraft trajectory optimization problem in the Earth-Moon system. The distributions depict costates in the space of their first three principal components (PC1, PC2 and PC3) at various stages of the diffusion process. An example trajectory corresponding to the realization of the data-point marked in red is shown below. Adapted from Ref.4
